_comment: Source: National EMS Scope of Practice Model 2019 including Change Notices 1.0 and 2.0 (August 2021, DOT HS 813 151). NHTSA/NASEMSO. This represents the MINIMUM national standard skill set for each provider level.
document_reference: National EMS Scope of Practice Model 2019: Including Change Notices 1.0 and 2.0 — NHTSA/NASEMSO, August 2021
note: State scopes of practice may be more restrictive or expansive than this national model. All interventions require education, national certification, state licensure, AND medical director credentialing.

EMR.description: First-responder level. Provides initial, basic, life-saving care while awaiting ALS arrival. Often a secondary job function (firefighter, law enforcement, industrial responder). Foundational assessment and life-safety skills.
airway_ventilation: Airway obstruction manual dislodgement techniques (back blows, abdominal thrusts)
medications: Auto-injector antidotes for chemical/hazardous material exposures
Epinephrine IM auto-injector for anaphylaxis (supplied/carried by EMS agency)
Naloxone intranasal — unit-dosed, premeasured (for suspected opioid overdose)
Opioid antagonist auto-injector for suspected opioid overdose

EMT.description: Primary scope for BLS ambulance transport. Builds on EMR with enhanced airway, medication, and assessment skills. Full-time primary job function. Transport-capable.
airway_ventilation: Oxygen — humidifiers, partial rebreather, simple face mask, Venturi mask
cardiovascular: Cardiac monitoring — 12-lead ECG acquisition and transmission
Telemetric monitoring and transmission of clinical data
medications: Aspirin — oral, for suspected ischemic chest pain (162-325 mg)
Nitroglycerin sublingual — patient's own prescribed medication, for suspected ischemic chest pain
OTC oral analgesics for pain or fever (acetaminophen, ibuprofen)
iv_fluids: IV maintenance — non-medicated IV fluids (maintenance of existing IV access only; not initiation)

AEMT.description: Mid-level provider between EMT and Paramedic. Adds IV/IO access, select IV medications, and capnography. In areas without paramedic coverage, AEMT is the highest level of prehospital care.
airway_ventilation: End-tidal CO2 monitoring AND interpretation of waveform capnography
cardiovascular: Intraosseous (IO) access initiation — adult and pediatric
IV maintenance of non-medicated IV fluids
IV maintenance of medicated IV fluids
medications: Analgesics — parenteral (morphine, fentanyl, ketorolac) for pain
Epinephrine — IV/IO for cardiac arrest or anaphylaxis
Glucagon — IM/IV/IO for hypoglycemia or beta-blocker overdose
Nitroglycerin sublingual — agency stock, for suspected ischemic chest pain

Paramedic.description: Highest level of prehospital EMS provider. Full ALS scope. Invasive airway management, advanced cardiac interventions, medication administration via all routes, RSI, and pharmacological management of complex presentations.
airway_ventilation: Airway obstruction dislodgement by direct laryngoscopy
cardiovascular: Transvenous cardiac pacing — monitoring and maintenance
Cardiac monitoring — 12-lead ECG acquisition AND interpretation
Access indwelling catheters and implanted central IV ports
medications: Antidysrhythmics: amiodarone, lidocaine, adenosine, atropine IV, procainamide, magnesium
RSI agents: succinylcholine, rocuronium, ketamine, etomidate, midazolam
Vasopressors: epinephrine infusion, norepinephrine, dopamine, vasopressin
All AEMT medications plus full pharmacological formulary per state/MCA/agency

cascade_principle: Each level includes all skills of the level below it. A Paramedic can perform all EMT skills. An AEMT can perform all EMT skills. State/MCA scope may restrict OR expand upon this minimum national model.
important_notes: This model represents MINIMUM competencies — states may expand beyond this
All skill performance requires education, certification, licensure, AND medical director credentialing
Medical director authorization determines what specific medications and procedures are used within each level
Scope floors: a lower level in the cascade CANNOT expand scope beyond what the state base allows
Atropine IV: listed under Paramedic but some states authorize at AEMT level per local protocol
Nitroglycerin (agency stock): listed under AEMT but some states authorize at EMT level
RSI: Paramedic-only — requires specific training and medical director credentialing
IO access: AEMT and above nationally; some states authorize at EMT level
